coredata.dc:title: One-step preparation of carbon quantum dots/silver nanoclusters and ratio-fluorescence detection of mercury ions (II)
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers.

coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Hang
Li, Xiaopei
Yin, Pengyuan
Fang, Shuju
Ma, Jie
He, Yanzhi
Yang, Wenrong
Tan, Wei
Li, Shaoqing
coredata.dc:description: The widespread existence of heavy metal ions in the environmental samples can pose a tremendous threat to human health. Herein, a novel ratio-fluorescence probe consisting of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots capped silver nanoclusters (N, S-CQDs/AgNCs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal technique for monitoring mercury ions (Hg2 +). Upon addition of Hg2+, N, S-CQDs/AgNCs displayed a diminished fluorescence at 762 nm with simultaneous enhancement at 435 nm, attributing to the interaction between Hg2+ and AgNCs occurring a redox reaction. Based on a significant alteration, N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were successfully employed for the determination of Hg2+ within a linear range of 0.1–10 µM and a limit of detection at 0.037 µM. Furthermore, the developed probe was applied to monitor Hg2+ in real water. These satisfactory results indicated that it could be achieved sensing for Hg2+ and provided a potential platform for environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Silver nanoclusters
Ratiometric fluorescent probe
Mercury ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectra of the synthesized N, S-CQDs/AgNCs under different mass ratios of N, S-CQDs, and AgNO3. (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra and (c) excitation and emission spectra of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/AgNCs. (d) TEM image of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs (the blue and yellow dotted circles signing N, S-CQDs and AgNCs.). (e) DLS image of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs. (f) FT-IR spectra of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/AgNCs.
(a) Full XPS spectrum of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs. The spectra with high resolution of (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s, (e) S 2p, and (f) Ag 3d.
(a) Full XPS spectrum of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs after adding Hg2+. (b) The high-resolution spectra Ag 3d and (c) Hg 4f.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs at different concentrations (0.1 - 10 μM) of Hg2+. (b) Calibration curves of fluorescence response (I762/I435) of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs with 0.1 - 10 μM Hg2+. (c) The selectivity (yellow bars) and anti-interference (green bars) of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs.
Graphical overview of the fabrication of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs and Hg2 + detection.
Comparison of different sensing platform for Hg2+ in aqueous solution.
Comparison of fluorescence detection of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs and CVAAS results for the analysis of Hg2+ in real samples.

head.ce:title: One-step preparation of carbon quantum dots/silver nanoclusters and ratio-fluorescence detection of mercury ions (II)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hang Yu and Xiaopei Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread existence of heavy metal ions in the environmental samples can pose a tremendous threat to human health. Herein, a novel ratio-fluorescence probe consisting of N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots capped silver nanoclusters (N, S-CQDs/AgNCs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal technique for monitoring mercury ions (Hg2 +). Upon addition of Hg2+, N, S-CQDs/AgNCs displayed a diminished fluorescence at 762 nm with simultaneous enhancement at 435 nm, attributing to the interaction between Hg2+ and AgNCs occurring a redox reaction. Based on a significant alteration, N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were successfully employed for the determination of Hg2+ within a linear range of 0.1–10 µM and a limit of detection at 0.037 µM. Furthermore, the developed probe was applied to monitor Hg2+ in real water. These satisfactory results indicated that it could be achieved sensing for Hg2+ and provided a potential platform for environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal pollution from industrial production has long been a major environmental challenge due to its potent toxicity and widespread emissions. Among these contaminants, mercury stands out as a highly toxic heavy metal characterized by exceptional environmental mobility and bioaccumulation potential (Caglayan, 2018; Hua et al., 2020; Low et al., 2024). Divalent mercury ions (Hg2+), as the most common ionic form of mercury, can accumulate in aquatic organisms and ultimately affect human health (Zeng et al., 2022). Even if extremely low concentration levels, unfortunately, intake of Hg2+ can cause various types of damage to the brain, heart, kidneys, and may even result in permanent damage like central nervous system (Caglayan, 2018; Chen et al., 2019). Current traditional methods for detecting Hg2+, such as spectrophotometry (Al-Bagawi et al., 2017), atomic absorption spectrometry (Yavuz et al., 2018), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (Zheng et al., 2019), x-ray absorption spectrometry (Liu et al., 2016), and Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Serafimovski et al., 2008), etc., provide reliable quantitative analysis. However, real-time and in situ monitoring is difficult to achieve due to these methods face critical limitations, including complex procedures and high costs. Fluorescence-based analytical platforms are a promising alternative for the determination in aqueous solutions, owing to their cost-effectiveness, superior sensitivity, and fast detection.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), consisting of quasi-spherical carbon materials with sizes below 10 nm, as a new zero-dimensional nanomaterial, they have garnered significant interest for their unique features including tunable fluorescence, chemical stability, and easy synthesis (Huo et al., 2022; Tiwari et al., 2023; Yun et al., 2022). Nevertheless, CQDs without modification are greatly limited due to their sparse surface functional groups and colloidal instability (Chang et al., 2016). Therefore, appropriate chemical modification of CQDs based on heteroatom doping (e.g., N, S, P) can enhance their applications in detection. Similarly, silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) demonstrate unique properties and a wide range of application prospects including easy synthesis, excellent dispersibility, ease of functionalization, low toxicity, and high catalytic activity (Xiaolin et al., 2024; Yun-Peng et al., 2020). These have promoted its broad application in various fields such as catalysis, optics, and biomedicine (Liu et al., 2024). Some drawbacks of AgNCs are found to are prone to aggregation or oxidation under light, oxidation, or high-temperature conditions, leading to structural damage of fluorescence. Therefore, it is crucial to propose effective strategies to improve its colloidal stability (Chen et al., 2021).

Most fluorescence probes have been extensively studied by fluorescence response (enhancement/quenching). Unfortunately, these probes based on a single emission intensity may suffer from environmental variables, such as fluorescence intensity, temperature, pH, and probe concentration, leading to the assay results being unreliable (Wu et al., 2017). Ratiometric probes show detection signals by utilizing the intensity ratio at different fluorescence emission wavelengths. By calculating the ratio, the effects of these external factors can be eliminated via the self-calibrating approach, which can significantly enhance the signal-to-noise ratio and ensure precise and reliable quantification of the target substance. Consequently, ratiometric sensing platforms have been paid much attention. Recently, some reports have displayed the latest progress of ratio sensor based on CDs. For example, Li et al. utilized copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and nitrogen-rich QDs, by aggregation-induced emission (AIE) between Pb²⁺ and CuNCs, achieving Pb²⁺ detection within the concentration range of 0.010–2.5 mg L⁻¹ with LOD of 0.0031 mg L⁻¹ (Li et al., 2020). Zhang et al. synthesized a molecularly imprinted ratio-fluorescent probe using biomass CDs emitting in red/blue as the response/reference units, which successfully detected tyramine within a linear range of 1–60 μg/L with LOD of 1.3 μg/kg. The recoveries in real samples ranged from 79.74 % to 112.12 % (Zhang et al., 2024). These show that ratiometric fluorescence probes have become a powerful tool for biological and environmental analysis. In addition, both CQDs and AgNCs have excellent fluorescence properties, and their combination further endow fluorescence amplification, broaden the fluorescence range, and improve the detection sensitivity. Among them, CQDs can provide a good growth condition for the formation of nanoparticles with controllable size and uniform distribution. Taking advantage of the interaction between nanoparticles and CQDs, this composite material is being used with potential applications in environmental monitoring.

In this study, a ratio-nanoprobe (N, S-CQDs/AgNCs) was constructed for fluorescence detection of Hg2+ and shown an excellent detection capability ( Scheme 1). A one-step synthesis process was employed to fabricate N, S-CQDs/AgNCs using AgNO3, N, S-CQDs and ascorbic acid. During this process, the formation of AgNCs significantly quenched the initial blue emission fluorescence of the N, S-CQDs. With the addition of Hg2+, the AgNCs exhibited a decline in fluorescence intensity, accompanied by the revival of the N, S-CQDs. Relying on this change in fluorescence signals, a quantitative relationship was established between the fluorescence ratio and the concentration of Hg2+. In summary, N, S-CQDs/AgNCs provided a simple, rapid, and cost-effective sensing strategy for Hg2+ monitoring, and it will have a promising future for applications in water pollution monitoring.
The supplementary material provided a detailed description of the information.

According to the current report (Yin et al., 2022), N, S-CQDs were prepared via a microwave-assisted technique. 1.0 g each of both citric acid (CA) and glutathione (GSH) were mixed with ultrapure water (20 mL) in a beaker using ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. Subsequently, using a commercial microwave oven, the solution was heated at 540 W for 5 min. At the end of the reaction, ultrapure water (20 mL) was used to dissolve the synthesized product, and the supernatant was taken after ultrasound and centrifugation (12000 rpm, 15 min) to remove large aggregates. After freeze-drying treatment (-40 ℃, 48 h), N, S-CQDs powder was collected for spare use.

N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were fabricated through a single-step hydrothermal process. In brief, 0.5 mL of AgNO3 (5 mM) and 0.5 mL of N, S-CQDs (1 g/L) were separately added to a beaker containing 8 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was agitated at 400 rpm for 10 min. Following this step, 1 mL of 50 mM ascorbic acid (AA) was introduced to the mixture and subjected to further stirring for 5 min, and then the solution transitioned from colorless to milky white. The resulting mixture was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min) and filtered using a membrane filter (0.22 μm) to remove large molecular impurities. The final product (N, S-CQDs/AgNCs) was transferred to a brown bottle and stored at 4 °C for future use.

For fluorescence detection, 2.3 mL of potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP) buffer solution (50 mM, pH=4), and 0.3 mL of the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs solution were added and dispersed. Subsequently, stock solutions containing various levels of Hg2+ (0.1–10 µM) were added to the centrifuge tubes with a total volume of 3 mL. After incubating for 5 min, fluorescence measurements were performed with both widths of excitation and emission slits set to 5 nm. According to the above experimental conditions, Hg²⁺ was measured using N, S-CQDs/AgNCs on each day for a total duration of ten days, and then ten consecutive measurements of Hg²⁺ using N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were carried out on the same day and the reproducibility of the inter-day and intra-day measurements was assessed by calculating respective RSDs based on the results of the measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Liangwang Lake and Laoyu River (Kunming, China) served as the sources for lake and river water samples, respectively. Tap water was directly obtained. All water samples were filtered via a microporous membrane (0.22 μm). Afterward, the filtered water samples were used to prepare Hg2+ solutions with different concentrations (0, 10, 30, and 50 µM). The above Hg2+ solutions were added to the fluorescent probes, respectively. The fluorescence spectra were analyzed according to the method of Section 2.4. A series of Hg²⁺ standard solutions (1–10 μg/L) were prepared, and their corresponding peaks were measured using a cold vapor atomic absorption mercury analyzer to establish a linear relationship between peak intensity and Hg²⁺ concentration.

To better enhance the optical properties of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, the mass ratios between N, S-CQDs, and AgNO3 were optimized. As displayed in Fig. 1a, N, S-CQDs, and AgNO3 were prepared at varying mass ratios (1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, 1:5, 1:6). The fluorescence properties are evaluated based on the ratio of intensities between the two emission peaks (I762/I435) because the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs exhibits two different signals. According to Fig. 1a, the optimal I762/I435 ratio was attained when the mass proportion between N, S-CQDs, and AgNO₃ was maintained at 1:5. Therefore, a mass ratio of 1:5 was determined to be the optimal condition.

The UV-Vis spectra and fluorescence techniques were employed to investigate the optical characteristics of both N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/AgNCs. Fig. 1b shows the absorption characteristics peak of N, S-CQDs at 342 nm, attributing to n-π* transition within aromatic CC bonds (Zaib et al., 2022). Notably, in the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, the peak positions of N, S-CQDs shows a slight red shift and a decrease in intensity. This may be originate from synergistic coordination between electron-donating moieties on N, S-CQDs and silver nanocluster surfaces during assembly, demonstrating the establishment of stable heterointerfaces mediated by dual charge-transfer pathways and orbital hybridization effects (Tran et al., 2025). In the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs system, no significant surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak is observed within 400–500 nm, indicating the formation of AgNCs rather than larger nanoparticles. This phenomenon can be attributed to the quantum confinement effect, where the restricted motion of electrons leads to the discretization of energy levels, resulting in a broad absorption peak in the spectrum instead of a distinct SPR peak (Wen et al., 2012). In addition, as shown in Fig. 1c, upon excitation at 371 nm, the fluorescence spectra reveal that N, S-CQDs exhibit the strongest emission peak at 442 nm. Upon the formation of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, no new excitation peak appeared. However, the emission peak changed from the original 442 nm to dual emission peaks at 435 and 762 nm. Compared with N, S-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N, S-CQDs/AgNCs shows a substantial reduction, indicating that the formation of the composite leads to fluorescence quenching, while the emissive centers of N, S-CQDs remained unaffected. Simultaneously, the positions of two emission peaks are far apart, suggesting the potential of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs as a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe. These results corresponded to the double emitting AgNCs in a previous report (Zhang and Fan, 2021).

The synthesized N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were analyzed for their morphological features and size distribution through TEM and DLS techniques. The N, S-CQDs/AgNCs display a high degree of crystallinity (Fig. 1d), with interplanar distances measured at 2.3 Å and 2.9 Å, corresponding to the (111) crystal plane spacing of AgNCs (Chen et al., 2023) and the (002) lattice plane of N, S-CQDs (Song et al., 2023), respectively. Fig. 1e shows that N, S-CQDs/AgNCs have a grateful shape and dispersion in size with an average of 8.42 ± 0.41 nm. In addition, the surface functional groups of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1f). The absorption feature at 3394 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds (Lu et al., 2016). Stretching vibrations of C-H groups is identified at 2938 cm−1. The tensile vibration of S-H at 2566 cm−1 in N, S-CQDs is visible (Riaz and Park, 2022; Zhao et al., 2019). The absorption peaks at 1710 cm−1, 1547 cm−1, and 1397 cm−1 are evidenced in C-H, CO, and C-N groups (Salman et al., 2023; Shahat et al., 2015; Zhan et al., 2019). The characteristic bands at 1209 cm−1 and 901 cm−1 are designated as C-S stretching vibrations and para-substituents on the benzene ring (Cheng et al., 2024; Travlou et al., 2017). Furthermore, compared with N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, the N-H peak in N, S-CQDs becomes narrower and the S-H disappears at 2566 cm−1, suggesting that the interaction between N, S-CQDs and AgNCs may be related to the N-H and S-H group.

To determine the chemical composition of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, XPS spectroscopy was employed to investigate their functional groups. The XPS spectra of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs are illustrated in Fig. 2. There are five peaks, belonging to O 1 s, N 1 s, Ag 3d, C 1 s, and S 2p, respectively (Fig. 2a). The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum reveals three distinct peaks located at C-C/CC (284.36 eV), C-N (287.12 eV), and CO (289.23 eV) (Fig. 2b). Two peaks appear in the O 1 s spectrum at 532.47 and 533.74 eV, which include CO and C-O (Fig. 2c). Two peaks of Ag-N (397.88 eV) and C-N (401.23 eV) appear on the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d). In Fig. 2e, the XPS spectrum of S 2p at 164.12 eV, 168.24 eV and 172.28 eV, which are deconvoluted into the binding energies of Ag-S, S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. Besides, the successful preparation of AgNCs can be easily confirmed from Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 2f). The peaks at 368.38 and 377.38 eV correspond to Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 levels, and both show the presence of Ag (0) and Ag (I) peaks (Maiti et al., 2016). Hence, FT-IR and XPS analyses verified the presence of covalent bonds between N, S-CQDs and AgNCs, specifically through Ag-N and Ag-S linkages.

The stability of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs was assessed across varying conditions. Firstly, the impact of pH on the I762/I435 ratio of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S1a, the I762/I435 ratio exhibits minimal variation across a broad pH ranging from 3 to 12, indicating that it is applicable at a broad pH condition. Next, the effect of temperature was examined under different conditions (20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C). In Fig. S1b, the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs maintain a stable fluorescence intensity ratio. Then, the effect of ionic strength was explored by adding varying concentrations of NaCl solutions (0.2–1 M). N, S-CQDs/AgNCs also remained stable, which denotes that it has outstanding salt tolerance (Fig. S1c). Last but not least, to evaluate the storage time of stability of the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, it was stored under room temperature for different periods of time. From Fig. S1d, there is almost no change in the intensity ratio after storage for 10 days, indicating that N, S-CQDs/AgNCs have excellent stability for a long time. The above results demonstrate that N, S-CQDs/AgNCs are stable at different pH, temperature, ionic strength and storage time.

When Hg²⁺ was introduced into the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs system, it initially triggered the release of Ag⁺ from the surface of the AgNCs, resulting in the disruption of the nanoclusters’ structural integrity. Concurrently, the N, S-CQDs, which were originally anchored to the AgNCs surface via interfacial interactions, were also released from their binding sites. This led to a significant enhancement of the intensity of their characteristic fluorescence peaks at 435 nm. Further spectral analysis revealed that the fluorescence enhancement at 435 nm was negatively correlated with the fluorescence burst at 762 nm in this system (Fig. 4a). This observation can be ascribed to a redox interaction occurring between the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs and Hg2+ (Lu, 2022), as described in the following equation:
N, S-CQDs/AgNCs + Hg2+→ N, S-CQDs/Agn-2NCsHg + 2Ag+

Furthermore, the redox potential of the Hg²⁺/Hg (0.85 V) exceeds that of the Ag⁺/Ag system (0.80 V) (Lu, 2022), it provides the necessary conditions for the spontaneous redox reactions. To further substantiate this reaction mechanism, the conversion of Ag (0) to Ag (I) was evidenced by XPS analysis ( Fig. 3b), demonstrating that the addition of Hg²⁺ induces the release of Ag⁺ from the surface of AgNCs, accompanied by the disruption of the AgNCs ' structural integrity. Meanwhile, in the Hg 4 f spectrum (Fig. 3c), the presence of Hg 4 f0 is observed, and the emergence of the Hg0 signal further confirms the occurrence of the aforementioned redox reaction. Therefore, the observed change in fluorescence ratio upon the addition of Hg²⁺ is ascribed to the redox reaction involving Hg²⁺ and the AgNCs.

The optimized N, S-CQDs/AgNCs sensors were employed for Hg²⁺ detection. In Fig. 4a, with the increase of Hg²⁺ concentration (0.1–10 μM), the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs at 435 nm increases. On the contrary, the fluorescence signal at 762 nm declines correspondingly. A significant linear correlation is observed between the ratio (I762/I435) and Hg²⁺ concentration (0.1–10 μM) (Fig. 4b). The regression equation is determined to be I
762
/I
435 = -0.035 ×[Hg²⁺]+ 0.9821 (R²=0.9895). Subsequently, LOD for Hg²⁺ is calculated to be 0.037 μM, and the lower quantification limit (LQD) is 0.1221 μM, using the formula for detection limits (The expression 3σ/S, wherein σ represents the standard deviation of the blank sample, and S denotes the slope of the calibration curve). In comparison to other Hg²⁺ detection techniques listed in Table 1, the proposed probe demonstrates superior sensitivity, selectivity, and possesses a promising potential for detecting Hg²⁺ in water. Reproducibility is another important parameter for the suitability of fluorescence probe, therefore the inter-day and intra-day detection of Hg²⁺ were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S2, the relative standard deviations (RSD) of the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs were 2.64 % and 2.42 % for inter-day and intra-day, respectively, and all of these results were less than 5 %, indicating that the fluorescent probe had promising reproducibility and reliability for the determination of Hg²⁺.

To evaluate the selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform, various ions (3 μM) were added to the N, S-CQDs/AgNCs, including Cd2+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cr2O7 2-, MnO4 -, HPO4 2-, H2PO4 -, NH4 +, HSO3 -, SO4 2-, I-, Cl-, and Hg2+. It can be seen (Fig. 4c) that only Hg2+ causes a significantly enhancement of I0/I (I0 and I represented the intensity ratio (I765/I435) of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs with the addition of Hg2+ in the absence and presence of interferences, respectively), demonstrating that the prepared N, S-CQDs/AgNCs had a good specific recognition ability for Hg2+. Moreover, the anti-interference ability was measured for Hg2+ recognition. Hg2+ and the above ions were acceded to N, S-CQDs/AgNCs according to the concentration ratio of 1:5, respectively. Other ions did not affect the detection of Hg2+ (Fig. 4c). In summary, this method owns superior selective and anti-interference for specific recognition of Hg2+.

In conclusion, we developed a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on N, S-CQDs/AgNCs for specific detection of Hg2+. The preparation process was simpler than traditional ratiometric probes, and it possessed high stability and superior optical properties. This detection mechanism was an oxidation-reduction interaction of Hg²⁺ and AgNCs, which led to the formation of a new fluorescent complex. The fluorescence signals of N, S-CQDs/AgNCs at 762 nm decreased, with the increase at 435 nm after the addition of Hg2+. A remarkable method for the determination of Hg2+ was proposed, and showed good sensitivity and selectivity with a linear range of 0.1–10 μM and LOD of 0.037 μM. This sensing platform was also applied to detect Hg2+ in actual a water and the results are in good agreement with the concentrations determined by cold vapor atomic absorption spectroscopy (CVAAS). This proposed method offers a facile strategy for Hg2+ monitoring in water environment.

Xiaopei Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Shuju Fang: Supervision. Pengyuan Yin: Validation. Yanzhi He: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jie Ma: Supervision. Wei Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Wenrong Yang: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shaoqing Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hang Yu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Yunnan Province (No. 202501AT070361), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 22266036), YMU-DEAKIN International Associated Laboratory on Functional Materials, Education Department of Yunnan Province [No. 117-02001001002107], and College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Project of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.psep.2025.107423.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Al-Bagawi et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simple and low cost dual-wavelength β-correction spectrophotometric determination and speciation of Mercury(II) in water using chromogenic reagent 4-(2-thiazolylazo) resorcinol
Spectrochim. Acta Part A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Al-Bagawi, A.H., Ahmad, W., Saigl, Z.M., Alwael, H., Al-Harbi, E.A., El-Shahawi, M.S., 2017. A simple and low cost dual-wavelength β-correction spectrophotometric determination and speciation of mercury(II) in water using chromogenic reagent 4-(2-thiazolylazo) resorcinol.
